quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-proportion z-test"?

options.text: (O - E)/√E for each cell; values beyond ±2 indicate that cell contributes notably to the overall χ² statistic
The probability of correctly rejecting H₀ when Hₐ is true; Power = 1 - β; increased by larger n, larger effect size, or larger α
Tests H₀: p = p₀ using z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n) and the standard normal distribution
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-proportion z-test" is defined as: Tests H₀: p = p₀ using z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n) and the standard normal distribution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Test statistic"?

options.text: z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n); measures how many standard errors the sample proportion falls from the hypothesized value
A variable that places individuals into groups or categories (e.g., gender, political party, blood type) and is summarized using counts or proportions
n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s
A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events

quiz_questions.explanation: "Test statistic" is defined as: z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n); measures how many standard errors the sample proportion falls from the hypothesized value. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-sided vs. two-sided test"?

options.text: One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)
A statistically significant slope may have little practical importance if the effect is very small; always consider R² and the magnitude of b₁
The line ŷ = a + bx that minimizes the sum of squared residuals; passes through the point (x̄, ȳ)
The probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value, assuming H₀ is true

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-sided vs. two-sided test" is defined as: One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conditions for z-test"?

options.text: A variable that places individuals into groups or categories (e.g., gender, political party, blood type) and is summarized using counts or proportions
A false positive incorrectly identifies a condition as present; a false negative incorrectly identifies it as absent
P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur
Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conditions for z-test" is defined as: Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Conclusion in context"?

options.text: μ = 1/p; the expected number of trials needed to achieve the first success
For Y = a + bX: E(Y) = a + b·E(X) and Var(Y) = b²·Var(X); adding a constant shifts the mean but does not change spread
State the decision (reject/fail to reject H₀) and interpret in the real-world context of the problem, including the p-value
Tests H₀: p₁ = p₂ using pooled proportion p̂c and z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/SE_pooled

quiz_questions.explanation: "Conclusion in context" is defined as: State the decision (reject/fail to reject H₀) and interpret in the real-world context of the problem, including the p-value. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Tests H₀: p = p₀ using z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n) and the standard normal distribution"?

options.text: Percentile
One-proportion z-test
Residual plot
Margin of error

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-proportion z-test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n); measures how many standard errors the sample proportion falls from the hypothesized value"?

options.text: Confounding variable
Test statistic
Variance (s²)
Zero in the interval

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Test statistic".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)"?

options.text: One-sided vs. two-sided test
Quantitative variable
Degrees of freedom (regression)
Mutually exclusive events

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-sided vs. two-sided test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Random sample or random assignment, n < 10% of population, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10"?

options.text: Conditions for z-test
Degrees of freedom (df)
Sample proportion (p̂)
Union and intersection

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conditions for z-test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "State the decision (reject/fail to reject H₀) and interpret in the real-world context of the problem, including the p-value"?

options.text: Large Counts condition (two samples)
Median
Conclusion in context
Chi-square statistic

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Conclusion in context".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Significance Tests for Proportions is accurate?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.
The slope b = r(sy/sx) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x, while the y-intercept a gives the predicted y when x = 0.
Significance Tests for Proportions test whether a population proportion p equals a hypothesized value p₀ using the test statistic z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n).
Binomial and Geometric Distributions model two important types of count data.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Significance Tests for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: The four-step process involves stating hypotheses, checking conditions (random sample, 10% condition, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10), calculating the test statistic and p-value, and stating a conclusion in context.
This distribution forms the basis for inference about population proportions using z-procedures.
Simple random sampling (SRS) gives every possible sample of size n an equal chance of selection, while stratified, cluster, and systematic sampling offer advantages in specific contexts.
Measures of center include the mean (arithmetic average sensitive to outliers), the median (resistant middle value), and the mode.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Significance Tests for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: One-sided tests (Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀) and two-sided tests (Hₐ: p ≠ p₀) require different p-value calculations.
The complement rule (P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A)), addition rule for unions, and multiplication rule for intersections form the core probability toolkit.
The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Significance Tests for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: Sampling Methods are systematic procedures for selecting a subset of individuals from a population to draw conclusions about the whole.
Normal Distribution is a symmetric, bell-shaped continuous probability distribution defined by its mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ), and it serves as the most important distribution in statistics.
Significance Tests for Proportions test whether a population proportion p equals a hypothesized value p₀ using the test statistic z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n).

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Significance Tests for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: The addition rule for mutually exclusive events (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)) and the general addition rule (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)) handle unions, while the multiplication rule handles intersections.

Blocking groups experimental units by a known source of variability (such as age, gender, or location) before random assignment within each block, increasing the experiment's ability to detect treatment effects.
Controlling Variables involves design strategies to isolate the effect of the treatment variable by holding other variables constant, matching subjects, or using statistical adjustments.

The four-step process involves stating hypotheses, checking conditions (random sample, 10% condition, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10), calculating the test statistic and p-value, and stating a conclusion in context.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Significance Tests for Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads
Tire wear studies using matched-pairs design: placing competing tire brands on opposite sides of the same car to control for driving conditions
Combining manufacturing tolerances: if a bolt and nut are produced independently, the play in the assembled joint has variance equal to the sum of their individual variances
Analyzing biological measurements (human heights, birth weights) that naturally approximate normal distributions to identify atypical values

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: A manufacturer testing whether the defect rate exceeds the 3% industry standard (H₀: p = 0.03, Hₐ: p > 0.03) to trigger quality interventions
Agricultural experiments blocking by field section (soil quality, sunlight exposure) before randomly assigning crop varieties within each block
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Significance Tests for Proportions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Pharmaceutical companies standardizing dosage timing, diet, and monitoring schedules across all treatment groups in a clinical trial
Public health officials testing whether the proportion of residents with elevated lead levels differs from the national rate to justify environmental remediation
Testing whether advertising spending significantly predicts sales revenue (H₀: β₁ = 0) to justify continued marketing budget allocation
Spam email filtering using conditional probability to classify messages based on the frequency of certain words in spam vs. legitimate emails (Bayesian classification)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Significance Tests for Proportions?

options.text: Market research testing whether product preference is associated with age group to determine whether different demographics need different marketing strategies
Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads
Demonstrating the CLT with simulations: rolling dice (uniform distribution) and plotting the mean of 30 rolls—the distribution of means is approximately normal despite the uniform population
Sociological research examining whether political party affiliation is independent of geographic region using survey data from a national random sample

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Significance Tests for Proportions in practice?

options.text: A manufacturer testing whether the defect rate exceeds the 3% industry standard (H₀: p = 0.03, Hₐ: p > 0.03) to trigger quality interventions
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Spam email filtering using conditional probability to classify messages based on the frequency of certain words in spam vs. legitimate emails (Bayesian classification)
A/B testing at tech companies: testing whether a new feature increases click-through rate, with H₀: no difference and p-value determining rollout decisions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Significance Tests for Proportions.
